abstract: Synthesizing unstructured research materials into manuscripts is an essential yet under-explored challenge in AI-driven scientific discovery. Existing autonomous writers are rigidly coupled to specific experimental pipelines, and produce superficial literature reviews. We introduce PaperOrchestra, a multi-agent framework for automated AI research paper writing. It flexibly transforms unconstrained pre-writing materials into submission-ready LaTeX manuscripts, including comprehensive literature synthesis and generated visuals, such as plots and conceptual diagrams. To evaluate performance, we present PaperWritingBench, the first standardized benchmark of reverse-engineered raw materials from 200 top-tier AI conference papers, alongside a comprehensive suite of automated evaluators. In side-by-side human evaluations, PaperOrchestra significantly outperforms autonomous baselines, achieving an absolute win rate margin of 50%-68% in literature review quality, and 14%-38% in overall manuscript quality.

citations.fullCitation: Y. Yamada, R. T. Lange, C. Lu, S. Hu, C. Lu, J. Foerster, J. Clune, and D. Ha. The ai scientist-v2: Workshop-level automated scientific discovery via agentic tree search. arXiv preprint arXiv:2504.08066, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper is the primary state-of-the-art baseline against which PaperOrchestra is benchmarked. It is repeatedly mentioned throughout the experiments as the main competitor, making it crucial for contextualizing the performance and contributions of the PaperOrchestra framework.
citations.title: The ai scientist-v2: Workshop-level automated scientific discovery via agentic tree search

Its importance is underscored by an ablation study (Figure 4) that demonstrates significant quality improvements, showing it is a core component of the proposed framework.
citations.title: Agentreview: Exploring peer review dynamics with llm agents

citations.justification: This paper provides one of the two main AI-based peer review frameworks used to holistically evaluate the technical quality and simulated acceptance rate of the manuscripts generated by PaperOrchestra. It is a key tool for the experimental validation of the main paper's claims.
citations.title: Scholarpeer: A context-aware multi-agent framework for automated peer review

citations.justification: PaperBanana is explicitly identified as the default module for the Plotting Agent in PaperOrchestra. This component is responsible for generating conceptual diagrams and plots, a key feature that distinguishes PaperOrchestra from other systems, making this citation integral to the framework's architecture.
citations.title: Paperbanana: Automating academic illustration for ai scientists

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Analysis: PaperOrchestra: A Multi-Agent Framework for Automated AI Research Paper Writing

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The authors of this research paper are Yiwen Song, Yale Song, Tomas Pfister, and Jinsung Yoon. All authors are affiliated with Google.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The work presented in "PaperOrchestra" addresses a challenge within the evolving landscape of AI-driven scientific discovery: the translation of unstructured research materials into structured, submission-ready manuscripts. This endeavor is contextualized by the increasing role of Large Language Models (LLMs) in transitioning AI from an assistive tool to an active participant in scientific processes (Eger et al., 2025).

Existing approaches to automating scientific research and writing generally fall into two categories:

*   **End-to-End Autonomous Research Frameworks:** Recent works such as AI Scientist-v1 (Lu et al., 2024), AI Scientist-v2 (Yamada et al., 2025), Cycle Researcher (Weng et al., 2024), and OmniScientist (Shao et al., 2025) have established the feasibility of automating entire research lifecycles. However, the authors identify a limitation in these systems: their writing modules are rigidly coupled to their internal experimental loops. This design restricts their ability to function as standalone tools capable of processing arbitrary human-provided pre-writing materials. Furthermore, empirical evaluations of these systems indicate deficiencies in literature synthesis, often resulting in superficial reviews with limited citations and a lack of conceptual diagram generation capabilities (Beel et al., 2025; Tang et al., 2025b).
*   **Automated Writing and Literature Synthesis Systems:** Earlier systems like PaperRobot (Wang et al., 2019) focused on incremental text generation but lacked the capacity for complex, data-driven narratives. More recent LLM-based assistants (e.g., Prism by OpenAI, 2026; data-to-paper by Technion-Kishony Lab, 2024) demonstrate capabilities in stylistic refinement or translating structured analytical results but typically require structured inputs or human guidance, not supporting end-to-end manuscript generation from raw, unstructured data. Specialized frameworks for literature review generation (e.g., AutoSurvey2 by Wu et al., 2025; LiRA by Go et al., 2025) are effective for producing long-form surveys but often lack the contextual awareness necessary for drafting targeted "Related Work" sections that contrast prior research and motivate specific research gaps within a new paper.

The authors identify a critical gap in the existing literature: the absence of a robust, standalone system that can flexibly transform unconstrained, early-stage pre-writing materials into a complete, submission-ready LaTeX manuscript, including comprehensive literature synthesis and the generation of diverse visuals. Additionally, the lack of a standardized benchmark specifically designed for evaluating automated AI research paper writing independently further complicates assessment in this domain. PaperOrchestra positions itself to address these identified limitations by offering a multi-agent framework designed for autonomy, flexibility with unstructured inputs, and comprehensive content generation, evaluated against a newly introduced benchmark.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The central objective of this research is to develop an automated framework capable of transforming unstructured preliminary research materials into submission-ready AI research papers. This objective is motivated by several identified challenges and gaps in current AI-driven scientific discovery and writing:

*   **Bridging the Translation Gap in AI-Driven Discovery:** The increasing involvement of AI in scientific discovery necessitates efficient mechanisms for translating raw ideas and experimental logs into formal, rigorous manuscripts. Existing autonomous research frameworks often integrate writing as a component tightly coupled to their experimental pipelines, rendering them unsuitable for processing human-provided, unconstrained pre-writing inputs. The aim is to create a decoupled system that can serve as a versatile writing tool.
*   **Addressing Deficiencies in Literature Synthesis:** Current autonomous writing agents demonstrate limitations in generating comprehensive and contextually aware literature reviews. These systems often rely on basic keyword searches, leading to superficial reviews, insufficient citations, and a failure to effectively position novel research against prior work or identify critical research gaps. A key motivation is to develop a system that can synthesize deep, targeted literature reviews with high citation rigor.
*   **Enhancing Visual Content Generation:** Existing automated writing tools typically restrict visual outputs to code-generated data plots. However, research papers often require conceptual diagrams to explain methodologies and architectural designs. The research is motivated by the need for a system capable of autonomously generating both statistical plots and conceptual diagrams to augment the scientific narrative.
*   **Improving Overall Manuscript Quality and Coherence:** The goal extends beyond merely generating text to producing high-quality, technically coherent, and well-structured manuscripts that meet academic standards. This involves implementing iterative refinement processes to enhance clarity, presentation, and scientific soundness, mimicking aspects of human peer review.
*   **Establishing a Standardized Evaluation Benchmark:** A significant motivation is the absence of a standardized benchmark to objectively evaluate the performance of automated AI research paper writing systems. This lack hinders comparative analysis and progress in the field. The work aims to introduce such a benchmark to facilitate rigorous assessment.

In summary, the key objectives are:
*   To develop **PaperOrchestra**, a standalone, multi-agent framework for automated LaTeX manuscript generation from unstructured pre-writing materials, encompassing deep literature synthesis, conceptual and statistical diagram generation, and iterative content refinement.
*   To introduce **PaperWritingBench**, the first standardized benchmark dataset for AI research paper writing, created by reverse-engineering raw materials from 200 top-tier AI conference papers.
*   To demonstrate that **PaperOrchestra** achieves a higher quality of literature review synthesis and overall manuscript quality compared to existing autonomous baselines, as assessed through both automated and human evaluations.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology employed in this research centers on the development of the PaperOrchestra framework and a dedicated benchmark for its evaluation.

**4.1 Task Formulation**
The end-to-end AI research paper generation task is defined as a function that maps a set of unconstrained pre-writing materials to a complete submission package. The input components are:
*   **Idea Summary (I)**: A brief overview of the proposed methodology, contributions, and theoretical foundation.
*   **Experimental Log (E)**: A compilation of raw experimental results, ablation studies, and performance metrics.
*   **LaTeX Template (T)**: Conference-provided template files.
*   **Conference Guidelines (G)**: Specific requirements mandated by the target conference.
*   **Figures (F)**: An optional set of pre-existing visual assets. If none are provided (F = ∅), the system autonomously synthesizes them.
The output is a finalized submission package (𝑃), consisting of the source LaTeX file (𝑃\_tex) and the rendered PDF (𝑃\_pdf).

**4.2 Dataset Construction: PaperWritingBench**
To evaluate the proposed pipeline, the authors introduced PaperWritingBench, a dataset comprising 200 accepted papers (100 each from CVPR 2025 and ICLR 2025). The dataset aims to isolate the writing task by providing reverse-engineered raw materials. The construction pipeline involves:
*   **Paper Acquisition and Extraction**: Papers were randomly sampled from OpenReview (ICLR) and CVF Open Access (CVPR). Raw PDFs were processed using MinerU (Wang et al., 2024) to generate structured Markdown and PDFFigures 2.0 (Clark and Divvala, 2016) to extract visual entities and captions.
*   **Synthesizing Raw Materials**: An LLM (Gemini-3.1-Pro) was prompted to reverse-engineer two core components from the extracted PDF content, ensuring full anonymization and self-containment (stripping citations, URLs, and explicit figure/table references) to prevent information leakage.
    *   **Idea Summary (I)**: Distilled the core methodology without experimental results. Two variants were created: Sparse (high-level ideas) and Dense (retaining formal definitions and LaTeX equations).
    *   **Experimental Log (E)**: Extracted experimental setup and empirical findings. Visual insights were converted into standalone factual observations.

**4.3 PaperOrchestra Framework**
PaperOrchestra is a multi-agent framework designed to transform pre-writing materials into submission-ready manuscripts through five sequential steps:
1.  **Outline Generation**: An **Outline Agent** synthesizes the input materials into a JSON outline. This outline includes a visualization plan (dictating plot types, data sources, aspect ratios), a targeted literature search strategy (macro-level context for the introduction, micro-level methodology clusters for related work), and a section-level writing plan with content bullets and citation hints for external dependencies.
2.  **Plot Generation**: A **Plotting Agent** executes the visualization plan. It utilizes PaperBanana (Zhu et al., 2026), a module that employs a closed-loop refinement system where a Vision-Language Model (VLM) critic iteratively evaluates and revises generated images and captions to resolve visual artifacts and ensure context awareness. This step operates in parallel with Step 3.
3.  **Literature Review**: A **Literature Review Agent** implements a hybrid discovery pipeline. It uses an LLM with web search to identify candidate papers and the Semantic Scholar API to authenticate their existence, fetch metadata, and enforce temporal cutoffs. After deduplication, it compiles a citation registry, auto-generates a BibTeX file, and drafts the Introduction and Related Work sections. This step operates in parallel with Step 2.
4.  **Section Writing**: A **Section Writing Agent** drafts the remaining core sections, including the abstract, methodology, experiments, and conclusion. It extracts numerical data from the experimental log to construct tables and integrates the generated figures, guided by the section-level outline and compiled citation bank.
5.  **Iterative Content Refinement**: A **Content Refinement Agent** iteratively optimizes the manuscript using simulated peer-review feedback. It leverages AgentReview (Jin et al., 2024) to evaluate the draft. Revisions are accepted if the overall score improves or ties with non-negative sub-axis gains; otherwise, the previous version is restored, or the process halts upon reaching an iteration limit.

**4.4 Baselines**
PaperOrchestra's performance was benchmarked against two pipelines:
*   **Single Agent**: A monolithic LLM call processing all raw materials for end-to-end drafting and bibliography construction.
*   **AI Scientist-v2** (Yamada et al., 2025): A state-of-the-art system featuring multi-round citation gathering, VLM-guided plot refinement, and iterative self-reflection.
For fair comparison, visual inputs were standardized to ground-truth figures, and the sparse idea setting was used as the standard input. All evaluated writing pipelines employed a universal anti-leakage prompt to mitigate pre-training data memorization.

**4.5 Evaluation Metrics (Autoraters)**
Automated evaluators were used to assess manuscript quality:
*   **Citation F1**: Measures citation coverage by comparing generated references against ground-truth references, categorized into P0 (Must-Cite) and P1 (Good-to-Cite) by an LLM. Precision, Recall, and F1 scores were calculated.
*   **Literature Review Quality**: An LLM evaluator (Gemini-3.1-Pro, GPT-5) scored the Introduction and Related Work sections across six axes (coverage, relevance, critical analysis, positioning, organization, citation rigor), grounded against venue-specific average citation counts.
*   **Overall Quality**: Two AI-based peer review frameworks, AI Scientist-v2 Reviewer (Yamada et al., 2025) and ScholarPeer (Goyal et al., 2026), provided multi-axis scores, overall ratings, and simulated acceptance decisions.
*   **Side-by-Side (SxS) Comparison**: Two automated LLM evaluators performed pairwise comparisons on Literature Review Quality and Overall Paper Quality across multiple axes, with positional bias mitigation.

**4.6 Human Evaluation**
To validate automated metrics, 11 AI researchers conducted blind SxS comparisons of 40 randomly sampled papers. They evaluated PaperOrchestra against baselines (Human GT, Single Agent, AI Scientist-v2) across Literature Review Quality and Overall Paper Quality dimensions.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments demonstrated PaperOrchestra's capabilities in automated AI research paper writing, particularly in literature synthesis and overall manuscript quality, relative to existing methods and human-written ground truth.

**5.1 Side-by-Side (SxS) Comparison**
Automated SxS evaluations (Figure 2) using LLM judges (Gemini-3.1-Pro and GPT-5) showed PaperOrchestra consistently outperformed autonomous baselines.
*   **Literature Review Quality**: PaperOrchestra achieved absolute win margins of 88%–99% against the Single Agent and AI Scientist-v2 baselines.
*   **Overall Paper Quality**: PaperOrchestra surpassed all AI competitors with win margins of 39%–86% over AI Scientist-v2 and 52%–88% over the Single Agent baseline. Human (GT) manuscripts consistently served as the upper bound.

**5.2 Technical Quality**
Evaluations using AI-based peer review frameworks (Table 2) further supported PaperOrchestra's performance.
*   **Simulated Acceptance Rates**: Under the ScholarPeer framework, PaperOrchestra achieved high simulated acceptance rates of 84% for CVPR and 81% for ICLR papers, closely aligning with Human (GT) baselines (86% and 94% respectively). This performance represented absolute acceptance gains of 13% (CVPR) and 9% (ICLR) over the strongest autonomous baseline.
*   **Multi-Axis Scores**: PaperOrchestra demonstrated superior scores across critical sub-axes such as Clarity, Presentation, and Soundness, indicating the generation of structurally coherent and technically sound manuscripts.

**5.3 Citation Coverage**
Analysis of Citation F1 scores (Table 3) indicated PaperOrchestra's effectiveness in mitigating under-citation.
*   **Citation Count**: PaperOrchestra generated 45.73–47.98 citations on average, closely mirroring Human (GT) writeups (approximately 59 citations). In contrast, autonomous baselines had significantly lower citation counts (9–14).
*   **Recall for P0 and P1 Citations**: PaperOrchestra improved P0 (Must-Cite) Recall by 2.13%–6.07% and P1 (Good-to-Cite) Recall by 12.59%–13.75% over the strongest baselines. This suggests a broader exploration of the academic landscape rather than a focus on only obvious references.

**5.4 Literature Review Quality**
LLM-as-a-Judge evaluations (Table 4) on literature review quality demonstrated PaperOrchestra's ability to synthesize analytical narratives.
*   **Overall Score**: PaperOrchestra achieved absolute Overall Score gains of 32.87%–33.25% (Gemini-3.1-Pro) and 9.66%–9.85% (GPT-5) over the strongest AI baseline, with scores comparable to Human (GT) baselines.
*   **Specific Axes**: It performed well in Citation Practices and Critical Analysis, indicating a capacity for analytical, well-grounded narrative generation rather than generic summaries.

**5.5 Human Evaluation**
Human SxS evaluations (Figure 3a) validated the automated metrics and confirmed PaperOrchestra's superior performance.
*   **Correlation**: Human preferences correlated with the GPT-5 evaluator for Overall Quality (Pearson r = 0.6458, Spearman ρ = 0.6355).
*   **Win Rates**: Humans rated PaperOrchestra as outperforming AI baselines with absolute win rate margins of 50%–68% for Literature Review Quality and 14%–38% for Overall Quality. The system also achieved a 43% tie/win rate against human-written ground truth in literature synthesis.

**5.6 Ablation Studies**
*   **Sparse vs. Dense Idea Input** (Table 5): Dense idea input generally led to better Overall Paper Quality (43%–56% win rates) by facilitating more rigorous methodology generation. However, Literature Review Quality showed near-parity, indicating the Literature Review Agent's robustness in executing targeted searches regardless of input density.
*   **Autonomous Visual Generation (PlotOff vs. PlotOn)** (Table 6): The Plotting Agent demonstrated competitive performance, with PlotOn (autonomously generated visuals) achieving ties or wins in 51%–66% of SxS matchups against PlotOff (human-authored GT figures), despite PlotOff having access to inherent information advantages from human-curated visuals.
*   **Importance of Content Refinement Agent** (Figure 4): Refined manuscripts (AfterRefine) consistently dominated unrefined drafts (BeforeRefine) with 79%–81% win rates and 0% losses. This iterative refinement loop led to substantial gains in simulated acceptance rates (+19% CVPR, +22% ICLR) and overall scores, highlighting its critical role in producing submission-ready manuscripts.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The PaperOrchestra framework and the accompanying PaperWritingBench dataset represent a contribution to the field of AI-driven scientific discovery and academic publishing.

**Advancement in Automated Scientific Writing:** PaperOrchestra addresses the challenge of translating unstructured preliminary research materials into formal, submission-ready LaTeX manuscripts. By developing a multi-agent framework capable of synthesizing deep literature reviews and generating diverse visuals (both plots and conceptual diagrams), the work expands the scope of AI's role in academic content creation beyond mere text generation or stylistic refinement.

**Bridging Key Gaps in the Research Landscape:** The work directly addresses the limitations of existing autonomous research frameworks, which are often rigidly coupled to experimental loops and produce superficial literature. It also improves upon existing automated writing tools that typically require structured inputs or lack the contextual awareness for targeted literature synthesis. This decoupled, standalone approach offers increased flexibility and utility for human researchers.

**Standardized Evaluation and Accelerated Research:** The introduction of PaperWritingBench, the first standardized benchmark for evaluating AI research paper writing, provides a crucial resource for the community. This benchmark facilitates objective comparison of different systems, enables clearer progress tracking, and can accelerate the development of more sophisticated automated writing agents.

**Enhancement of Manuscript Quality and Rigor:** The demonstrated performance of PaperOrchestra, particularly its superior ability in literature review synthesis, citation practices, and overall manuscript quality compared to autonomous baselines, suggests the potential for AI systems to generate technically sound and academically rigorous papers. The iterative refinement loop is identified as a key component in elevating drafts to submission-ready quality.

**Assistive Tool for Researchers:** While not positioned as a replacement for human authorship, PaperOrchestra functions as an advanced assistive tool. By automating the laborious drafting process, including literature searching, citation management, content structuring, and initial visual generation, it could allow human researchers to allocate more time to scientific ideation, experimentation, and critical analysis. The competitive computational latency further supports its practical applicability.

**Future Directions in Human-AI Collaboration:** The research indicates potential for future developments, such as incorporating richer research artifacts (e.g., experimental code, raw data files) and transitioning these autonomous agents into interactive, dynamic writing environments. This could foster more seamless and effective human-AI scientific collaboration, enhancing productivity and the quality of scientific output.

**Ethical Considerations:** The authors explicitly acknowledge the ethical implications, positioning the system as an assistive tool where human researchers retain full accountability for factual accuracy, originality, and validity. Safeguards against hallucination are discussed, underscoring the ongoing responsibility of users to verify outputs and prevent the propagation of biases or misinformation.
overview:
The translation of research ideas and experimental findings into a formal, submission-ready manuscript is a resource-intensive stage of the scientific process. As Large Language Models (LLMs) take a more active role in scientific discovery, there is an increasing demand for systems that can automate this translation. While existing autonomous research frameworks often include writing components, these modules are typically tied to internal experimental loops, making them less effective for processing external, unstructured materials provided by human researchers. Furthermore, many current automated systems struggle with synthesizing comprehensive literature reviews or generating conceptual diagrams that explain complex methodologies.

PaperOrchestra is a multi-agent framework developed by researchers at Google to address these limitations. It functions as a standalone system capable of transforming unstructured pre-writing materials—such as high-level idea summaries and raw experimental logs—into complete LaTeX manuscripts. By decoupling the writing process from the experimental phase, the framework offers a flexible tool for researchers to convert early-stage outputs into structured academic papers.

## Task Formulation and Input Requirements

The end-to-end writing task is formulated as a mapping function that transforms a set of unconstrained inputs into a finalized submission package. The system processes five primary input components:

1.  **Idea Summary ($I$):** A brief overview of the methodology, theoretical foundations, and proposed contributions. This can be sparse (high-level) or dense (containing formal definitions).
2.  **Experimental Log ($E$):** A compilation of raw results, ablation studies, and performance metrics.
3.  **LaTeX Template ($T$):** The specific formatting files required by the target venue.
4.  **Conference Guidelines ($G$):** Mandatory requirements for submission.
5.  **Figures ($F$):** Optional pre-existing visual assets. If no figures are provided ($F = \emptyset$), the system autonomously synthesizes them.

The output is a finalized submission package $P$, which includes the source LaTeX file $P_{\text{tex}}$ and the rendered PDF $P_{\text{pdf}}$. The relationship is expressed as:

$$
P = f(I, E, T, G, F)
$$

## The Multi-Agent Architecture

PaperOrchestra utilizes a specialized multi-agent system where different agents handle specific aspects of the manuscript generation process. This modular approach allows for targeted optimization of complex tasks like literature search and visual synthesis.

### 1. Outline Agent
The process begins with the Outline Agent, which synthesizes the raw materials into a comprehensive JSON outline. This outline serves as the architectural blueprint for the paper. It includes a visualization plan (specifying plot types and data sources), a literature search strategy (defining macro-level context for the introduction and micro-level clusters for the related work), and a section-level plan with content bullets and citation hints.

### 2. Plotting Agent (PaperBanana)
Visual content is generated by the Plotting Agent using a module called PaperBanana. Unlike systems that only produce data plots from code, PaperOrchestra generates both statistical plots and conceptual diagrams (e.g., architectural diagrams). It employs a closed-loop refinement system where a Vision-Language Model (VLM) criticizes generated visuals and iteratively revises the code to resolve artifacts and ensure the images align with the text.

### 3. Literature Review Agent
A significant challenge in automated writing is the production of "Related Work" sections that are analytical rather than merely descriptive. The Literature Review Agent uses a hybrid discovery pipeline. It identifies candidate papers via web search and authenticates them using the Semantic Scholar API to retrieve metadata and enforce temporal cutoffs. It then compiles a citation registry, generates a BibTeX file, and drafts the Introduction and Related Work sections by positioning the current research against the existing landscape.

### 4. Section Writing Agent
This agent drafts the core technical sections, including the methodology, experiments, and conclusion. It extracts numerical data from the experimental log to construct LaTeX tables and integrates the figures generated by the Plotting Agent. The agent ensures that the narrative remains consistent with the section-level outline and the established citation bank.

### 5. Content Refinement Agent (AgentReview)
The final stage involves an iterative optimization process that mimics human peer review. The Content Refinement Agent uses a simulated review framework to evaluate the initial draft. Revisions are made based on feedback regarding clarity, presentation, and scientific soundness. A version control mechanism is implemented where revisions are only accepted if they improve the overall score; otherwise, the system reverts to a previous iteration or halts upon reaching a limit.

## PaperWritingBench: A New Standard for Evaluation

To rigorously assess the performance of PaperOrchestra, the researchers introduced PaperWritingBench. This benchmark dataset consists of 200 accepted papers from top-tier AI conferences (CVPR 2025 and ICLR 2025). 

The benchmark was created by reverse-engineering these papers into their constituent raw materials. An LLM was used to distill the methodology into idea summaries and extract findings into experimental logs while stripping away citations and explicit figure references. This process ensures that evaluation is focused strictly on the writing agent's ability to reconstruct a professional paper from raw data, without relying on the original author's narrative structure.

## Empirical Findings and Performance

PaperOrchestra was evaluated against several baselines, including a monolithic single-agent LLM and the state-of-the-art AI Scientist-v2 pipeline. The evaluation utilized both automated "autoraters" and blind human reviews by AI researchers.

### Literature Synthesis and Citation Rigor
One of the primary findings was the framework's ability to mitigate under-citation. Traditional autonomous systems often produce superficial reviews with very few citations. In contrast, PaperOrchestra generated an average of 45–48 citations per paper, approaching the human ground truth average of 59. 

In terms of citation coverage, the system showed significant improvements in recall for $P_0$ (Must-Cite) and $P_1$ (Good-to-Cite) references. The "Literature Review Quality" metric, which evaluates coverage, relevance, and critical analysis, showed that PaperOrchestra achieved absolute gains of over 30% compared to existing autonomous baselines.

### Overall Manuscript Quality
Using AI-based peer review frameworks, PaperOrchestra achieved simulated acceptance rates of 84% for CVPR and 81% for ICLR. These rates are comparable to the human-written ground truth papers in the benchmark. Side-by-side (SxS) comparisons conducted by LLM judges showed that PaperOrchestra won 88%–99% of matchups against baselines in literature review quality and 39%–86% in overall paper quality.

### Human Validation
Human evaluations correlated significantly with the automated metrics ($r = 0.6458$ and $\rho = 0.6355$ for overall quality). Human reviewers rated PaperOrchestra as superior to AI baselines in over 50% of literature review matchups. Notably, the system achieved a 43% tie or win rate when compared directly against the original human-authored manuscripts in literature synthesis, suggesting a high degree of technical coherence.

## The Role of Iterative Refinement

A critical component of the framework's success is the Content Refinement Agent. Ablation studies demonstrated that the refinement loop is essential for reaching submission-ready quality. Manuscripts that underwent iterative refinement dominated unrefined drafts in 80% of matchups. This step led to a 19–22% increase in simulated acceptance rates, underscoring the importance of self-critique and revision in the scientific writing process.

Furthermore, the autonomous visual generation module (PaperBanana) was found to be highly competitive. Despite the inherent difficulty of generating technical diagrams without human guidance, the Plotting Agent's outputs achieved ties or wins in 51%–66% of matchups against human-authored figures when evaluated for their ability to support the paper's narrative.

## Implications for the Research Community

PaperOrchestra represents a shift toward more flexible and high-fidelity automated scientific writing. By focusing on deep literature synthesis and visual diversity, it addresses the most common criticisms of current AI-generated content—namely, that it is often generic or visually sparse.

The framework is intended to serve as an assistive tool rather than a replacement for human researchers. It automates the structured drafting, formatting, and literature sourcing phases, potentially allowing researchers to focus on high-level conceptual work. However, the authors maintain that human accountability remains paramount. Users are responsible for verifying the accuracy of the experimental logs and the validity of the generated methodology to prevent the propagation of hallucinations or technical errors.

The introduction of PaperWritingBench provides the community with a standardized tool for ongoing research in this domain. Future developments may involve the integration of more diverse research artifacts, such as raw data files or experimental codebases, to further enhance the system's ability to generate precise and data-driven scientific narratives.

keyInsights: A multi-agent architecture with specialized roles (e.g., Outline, Plotting, Literature Review, Section Writing, Content Refinement Agents) is effective for transforming diverse unstructured inputs into structured, high-quality research papers.

Iterative content refinement, simulated through peer-review feedback, significantly improves manuscript quality, elevating drafts to submission-ready standards and substantially increasing simulated acceptance rates.

The use of a dedicated, reverse-engineered benchmark (PaperWritingBench) is crucial for rigorously evaluating the performance of automated paper writing systems, especially regarding literature synthesis and overall coherence.
originalProblem: Existing autonomous research frameworks are rigidly coupled to experimental loops, limiting their ability to process arbitrary human-provided pre-writing materials into standalone manuscripts.

Current automated writing agents often produce superficial literature reviews with insufficient citations and lack the capability to generate diverse visuals beyond data plots, such as conceptual diagrams.
A standardized benchmark for objectively evaluating automated AI research paper writing systems independently was missing, hindering comparative analysis and progress.
results: PaperOrchestra consistently outperformed autonomous baselines in automated side-by-side evaluations, achieving 88%–99% win margins for literature review quality and 39%–86% for overall paper quality against competitors.

It achieved high simulated acceptance rates (84% for CVPR, 81% for ICLR) under the ScholarPeer framework, with notable gains of 13% and 9% over the strongest autonomous baseline, and produced comprehensive citation lists mirroring human-written papers more closely.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of the iterative Content Refinement Agent, leading to 79%–81% win rates over unrefined drafts, and demonstrated the competitiveness of autonomously generated visuals against human-authored ground truth figures.
solution: Developed PaperOrchestra, a multi-agent framework designed for autonomous LaTeX manuscript generation from unstructured inputs, featuring sequential steps for outline generation, parallel plot and literature review generation, section writing, and iterative content refinement.

Introduced PaperWritingBench, a new benchmark dataset of 200 top-tier AI conference papers reverse-engineered into raw pre-writing materials (idea summaries, experimental logs) to isolate and evaluate the writing task.

Integrated a Plotting Agent with VLM-guided refinement for autonomous generation of both statistical plots and conceptual diagrams, and a Literature Review Agent employing a hybrid discovery pipeline for deep, targeted literature synthesis.
summary.summary: Google researchers developed PaperOrchestra, a multi-agent AI framework that converts unstructured pre-writing materials into submission-ready AI research papers, including deep literature reviews and diverse visuals. The system achieved simulated acceptance rates of 84% for CVPR and 81% for ICLR papers on a new benchmark, outperforming existing autonomous writing baselines.

title: PaperOrchestra: A Multi-Agent Framework for Automated AI Research Paper Writing
